Discussion 2.3: Interpret Principal Components¶

Principal Component 1: Socio-Economic Status & Housing

Top Weights: This component is heavily influenced by features like WEALTH, HH_EINKOMMEN_SCORE (estimated household income), and FINANZ_HAUSBAUER (homeownership) on one end, and features like MOBI_REGIO (movement patterns) and KBA05_ANTG1 (share of 1-2 family homes in the microcell) on the other.

Interpretation: This component represents a person's financial and social status. One direction of this axis points toward wealthy homeowners living in low-mobility, established neighborhoods. The opposite direction points toward lower-income individuals with higher mobility, living in smaller or more densely packed housing.

Principal Component 2: Age & Generational Financial Typology

Top Weights: This component contrasts ALTERSKATEGORIE_GROB (estimated age based on name) and FINANZ_VORSORGER (financial typology: "be prepared") against DECADE (generation), FINANZ_SPARER (financial typology: money saver), and FINANZ_UNAUFFAELLIGER (inconspicuous spender).

Interpretation: This component clearly represents Age and Generational attitudes. One side of the axis points toward older individuals who grew up in earlier decades and prioritize financial preparation. The opposite side points toward younger generations who are more likely to be active money-savers and cautious spenders.

Principal Component 3: Gender & Personality Traits

Top Weights: This component is strongly driven by ANREDE_KZ (Gender) alongside personality topologies (the SEMIO features). One side groups SEMIO_KAEM (combative attitude), SEMIO_DOM (dominant-minded), and SEMIO_KRIT (critical-minded). The other side groups SEMIO_SOZ (socially-minded), SEMIO_FAM (family-minded), and SEMIO_KULT (cultural-minded).

Interpretation: This component categorizes Gender and associated personality traits. It contrasts stereotypically male-associated traits in this dataset (combative, dominant, critical) against stereotypically female-associated traits (social, family-oriented, culturally engaged).

Step 3: Clustering¶

Step 3.1: Apply Clustering to General Population¶

You've assessed and cleaned the demographics data, then scaled and transformed them. Now, it's time to see how the data clusters in the principal components space. In this substep, you will apply k-means clustering to the dataset and use the average within-cluster distances from each point to their assigned cluster's centroid to decide on a number of clusters to keep.

  • Use sklearn's KMeans class to perform k-means clustering on the PCA-transformed data.
  • Then, compute the average difference from each point to its assigned cluster's center. Hint: The KMeans object's .score() method might be useful here, but note that in sklearn, scores tend to be defined so that larger is better. Try applying it to a small, toy dataset, or use an internet search to help your understanding.
  • Perform the above two steps for a number of different cluster counts. You can then see how the average distance decreases with an increasing number of clusters. However, each additional cluster provides a smaller net benefit. Use this fact to select a final number of clusters in which to group the data. Warning: because of the large size of the dataset, it can take a long time for the algorithm to resolve. The more clusters to fit, the longer the algorithm will take. You should test for cluster counts through at least 10 clusters to get the full picture, but you shouldn't need to test for a number of clusters above about 30.
  • Once you've selected a final number of clusters to use, re-fit a KMeans instance to perform the clustering operation. Make sure that you also obtain the cluster assignments for the general demographics data, since you'll be using them in the final Step 3.3.
In [31]:
# Over a number of different cluster counts...
from sklearn.cluster import MiniBatchKMeans
import numpy as np

# Take a random sample to plot the elbow curve without crashing RAM!
np.random.seed(42)
sample_indices = np.random.choice(azdias_pca.shape[0], size=50000, replace=False)
azdias_pca_sample = azdias_pca[sample_indices]

scores = []
# Testing 2, 4, 6... up to 20 clusters to find the elbow
centers = list(range(2, 21, 2)) 

print("Testing cluster counts on sample data... Please wait.")
for center in centers:
    # run k-means clustering on the data and...
    # Using MiniBatchKMeans to protect your memory!
    kmeans = MiniBatchKMeans(n_clusters=center, batch_size=5000, random_state=42)
    kmeans.fit(azdias_pca_sample)
    
    # compute the average within-cluster distances.
    # .score() returns a negative number, so we take the absolute value
    score = np.abs(kmeans.score(azdias_pca_sample))
    scores.append(score)
    print(f"Fitted {center} clusters. Score: {score}")

Testing cluster counts on sample data... Please wait.
Fitted 2 clusters. Score: 7252404.878317373
Fitted 4 clusters. Score: 6738689.69199987
Fitted 6 clusters. Score: 6466892.006293865
Fitted 8 clusters. Score: 6411487.608823533
Fitted 10 clusters. Score: 6239140.493429255
Fitted 12 clusters. Score: 6163342.257071185
Fitted 14 clusters. Score: 6041288.784052797
Fitted 16 clusters. Score: 5965514.782103964
Fitted 18 clusters. Score: 5878102.774244888
Fitted 20 clusters. Score: 5822343.436547705
In [32]:
# Investigate the change in within-cluster distance across number of clusters.
# HINT: Use matplotlib's plot function to visualize this relationship.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.plot(centers, scores, linestyle='--', marker='o', color='b', linewidth=2)
plt.xlabel('Number of Clusters (K)')
plt.ylabel('Average Within-Cluster Distance (SSE)')
plt.title('K-Means Clustering - Elbow Method')
plt.xticks(centers)
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.5)
plt.show()
In [33]:
# Re-fit the k-means model with the selected number of clusters and obtain
# cluster predictions for the general population demographics data.

# Replace 12 with whatever number looks like the "elbow" on your graph
chosen_k = 12

# Initialize the model using the chosen K and our memory-safe MiniBatch algorithm
kmeans_final = MiniBatchKMeans(n_clusters=chosen_k, batch_size=5000, random_state=42)

# fit_predict fits the model to the FULL data AND returns a list assigning every person to a cluster
print(f"Assigning {azdias_pca.shape[0]} people to {chosen_k} clusters...")
azdias_clusters = kmeans_final.fit_predict(azdias_pca)

print("Clustering complete! Every person is now assigned a demographic group.")

Assigning 797906 people to 12 clusters...
Clustering complete! Every person is now assigned a demographic group.

Discussion 3.1: Apply Clustering to General Population¶

(Double-click this cell and replace this text with your own text, reporting your findings and decisions regarding clustering. Into how many clusters have you decided to segment the population?)

Step 3.2: Apply All Steps to the Customer Data¶

Now that you have clusters and cluster centers for the general population, it's time to see how the customer data maps on to those clusters. Take care to not confuse this for re-fitting all of the models to the customer data. Instead, you're going to use the fits from the general population to clean, transform, and cluster the customer data. In the last step of the project, you will interpret how the general population fits apply to the customer data.

  • Don't forget when loading in the customers data, that it is semicolon (;) delimited.
  • Apply the same feature wrangling, selection, and engineering steps to the customer demographics using the clean_data() function you created earlier. (You can assume that the customer demographics data has similar meaning behind missing data patterns as the general demographics data.)
  • Use the sklearn objects from the general demographics data, and apply their transformations to the customers data. That is, you should not be using a .fit() or .fit_transform() method to re-fit the old objects, nor should you be creating new sklearn objects! Carry the data through the feature scaling, PCA, and clustering steps, obtaining cluster assignments for all of the data in the customer demographics data.
In [34]:
# Load in the customer demographics data.
# The instructions note this file is separated by semicolons, not commas!
customers = pd.read_csv('Udacity_CUSTOMERS_Subset.csv', sep=';')

print(f"Raw customer data shape: {customers.shape}")

Raw customer data shape: (191652, 85)
In [35]:
# Apply preprocessing, feature transformation, and clustering from the general
# demographics onto the customer data, obtaining cluster predictions for the
# customer demographics data.

import gc

print("1. Cleaning Customer Data...")
customers_clean = clean_data(customers)

# --- SAFETY NET: Align Columns ---
# Make sure the customer data has the exact same columns (and in the exact same order) 
# as the general population data before we run the math.
missing_cols = set(azdias_clean.columns) - set(customers_clean.columns)
for c in missing_cols:
    customers_clean[c] = 0
customers_clean = customers_clean[azdias_clean.columns]
# ---------------------------------

print("2. Imputing Missing Values (using pre-fit imputer)...")
# Notice we only use .transform()!
customers_imputed_matrix = imputer.transform(customers_clean)
customers_imputed = pd.DataFrame(customers_imputed_matrix, columns=customers_clean.columns)

print("3. Scaling Features (using pre-fit scaler)...")
# Notice we only use .transform()!
customers_scaled_matrix = scaler.transform(customers_imputed)
customers_scaled = pd.DataFrame(customers_scaled_matrix, columns=customers_imputed.columns)

print("4. Downcasting to save memory...")
customers_scaled_light = customers_scaled.astype(np.float32)

# Free up RAM before PCA
del customers_clean
del customers_imputed
del customers_imputed_matrix
del customers_scaled
del customers_scaled_matrix
gc.collect()

print("5. Applying PCA (using pre-fit PCA model)...")
# Notice we only use .transform()!
customers_pca = pca_final.transform(customers_scaled_light)

print("6. Predicting Clusters (using pre-fit KMeans model)...")
# Notice we only use .predict()!
customers_clusters = kmeans_final.predict(customers_pca)

print(f"SUCCESS! Customer data has been mapped to {len(np.unique(customers_clusters))} clusters.")

1. Cleaning Customer Data...
2. Imputing Missing Values (using pre-fit imputer)...
3. Scaling Features (using pre-fit scaler)...
4. Downcasting to save memory...
5. Applying PCA (using pre-fit PCA model)...
6. Predicting Clusters (using pre-fit KMeans model)...
SUCCESS! Customer data has been mapped to 12 clusters.

Step 3.3: Compare Customer Data to Demographics Data¶

At this point, you have clustered data based on demographics of the general population of Germany, and seen how the customer data for a mail-order sales company maps onto those demographic clusters. In this final substep, you will compare the two cluster distributions to see where the strongest customer base for the company is.

Consider the proportion of persons in each cluster for the general population, and the proportions for the customers. If we think the company's customer base to be universal, then the cluster assignment proportions should be fairly similar between the two. If there are only particular segments of the population that are interested in the company's products, then we should see a mismatch from one to the other. If there is a higher proportion of persons in a cluster for the customer data compared to the general population (e.g. 5% of persons are assigned to a cluster for the general population, but 15% of the customer data is closest to that cluster's centroid) then that suggests the people in that cluster to be a target audience for the company. On the other hand, the proportion of the data in a cluster being larger in the general population than the customer data (e.g. only 2% of customers closest to a population centroid that captures 6% of the data) suggests that group of persons to be outside of the target demographics.

Take a look at the following points in this step:

  • Compute the proportion of data points in each cluster for the general population and the customer data. Visualizations will be useful here: both for the individual dataset proportions, but also to visualize the ratios in cluster representation between groups. Seaborn's countplot() or barplot() function could be handy.
    • Recall the analysis you performed in step 1.1.3 of the project, where you separated out certain data points from the dataset if they had more than a specified threshold of missing values. If you found that this group was qualitatively different from the main bulk of the data, you should treat this as an additional data cluster in this analysis. Make sure that you account for the number of data points in this subset, for both the general population and customer datasets, when making your computations!
  • Which cluster or clusters are overrepresented in the customer dataset compared to the general population? Select at least one such cluster and infer what kind of people might be represented by that cluster. Use the principal component interpretations from step 2.3 or look at additional components to help you make this inference. Alternatively, you can use the .inverse_transform() method of the PCA and StandardScaler objects to transform centroids back to the original data space and interpret the retrieved values directly.
  • Perform a similar investigation for the underrepresented clusters. Which cluster or clusters are underrepresented in the customer dataset compared to the general population, and what kinds of people are typified by these clusters?
In [36]:
# Compare the proportion of data in each cluster for the customer data to the
# proportion of data in each cluster for the general population.
# 1. Define the original totals (to save you from reloading the raw CSVs into RAM!)
azdias_original_total = 891221
customers_original_total = 191652

# 2. Calculate how many rows were dropped due to missing data
azdias_dropped = azdias_original_total - len(azdias_clusters)
customers_dropped = customers_original_total - len(customers_clusters)

# 3. Create dictionaries counting the occurrences of each cluster
azdias_counts = pd.Series(azdias_clusters).value_counts().to_dict()
customers_counts = pd.Series(customers_clusters).value_counts().to_dict()

# Add the dropped rows as "Cluster -1"
azdias_counts[-1] = azdias_dropped
customers_counts[-1] = customers_dropped

# 4. Convert counts to proportions (percentages)
azdias_props = {k: v / azdias_original_total for k, v in azdias_counts.items()}
customers_props = {k: v / customers_original_total for k, v in customers_counts.items()}

# 5. Build a clean DataFrame for seaborn to plot
df_props = pd.DataFrame([azdias_props, customers_props], index=['General Population', 'Customers']).T
df_props.reset_index(inplace=True)
df_props.rename(columns={'index': 'Cluster'}, inplace=True)
df_props = df_props.melt(id_vars='Cluster', var_name='Dataset', value_name='Proportion')

# 6. Plot the comparison
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6))
sns.barplot(x='Cluster', y='Proportion', hue='Dataset', data=df_props, palette=['skyblue', 'salmon'], edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Proportion of Population in Each Cluster (Including Cluster -1 for Missing Data)')
plt.ylabel('Proportion of Dataset')
plt.xlabel('Cluster Number (-1 = Missing Data)')
plt.grid(True, axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
plt.show()
In [37]:
# What kinds of people are part of a cluster that is overrepresented in the
# customer data compared to the general population?
# Replace this with the cluster number where the red bar is tallest!
overrepresented_cluster = 3 

# 1. Grab the math coordinates for this cluster's center
centroid_pca = kmeans_final.cluster_centers_[overrepresented_cluster]

# 2. Reverse the PCA compression
centroid_scaled = pca_final.inverse_transform(centroid_pca)

# 3. Reverse the StandardScaler
centroid_original = scaler.inverse_transform([centroid_scaled])

# 4. Put it into a readable pandas Series
target_audience = pd.Series(centroid_original[0], index=azdias_clean.columns)

print("--- OVERREPRESENTED CLUSTER (Target Audience) ---")
# Print the most defining features of this group
print(target_audience[['ALTERSKATEGORIE_GROB', 'ANREDE_KZ', 'HH_EINKOMMEN_SCORE', 'WEALTH', 'LIFE_STAGE', 'DECADE', 'FINANZ_SPARER']])

--- OVERREPRESENTED CLUSTER (Target Audience) ---
ALTERSKATEGORIE_GROB     3.551973
ANREDE_KZ                1.568202
HH_EINKOMMEN_SCORE       4.108845
WEALTH                   2.622722
LIFE_STAGE               3.352464
DECADE                  61.291955
FINANZ_SPARER            1.397140
dtype: float64
In [38]:
# What kinds of people are part of a cluster that is underrepresented in the
# customer data compared to the general population?
# Replace this with the cluster number where the blue bar is tallest!
underrepresented_cluster = 5 

# Reverse the math
centroid_pca_under = kmeans_final.cluster_centers_[underrepresented_cluster]
centroid_scaled_under = pca_final.inverse_transform(centroid_pca_under)
centroid_original_under = scaler.inverse_transform([centroid_scaled_under])

non_target_audience = pd.Series(centroid_original_under[0], index=azdias_clean.columns)

print("--- UNDERREPRESENTED CLUSTER (Non-Target Audience) ---")
print(non_target_audience[['ALTERSKATEGORIE_GROB', 'ANREDE_KZ', 'HH_EINKOMMEN_SCORE', 'WEALTH', 'LIFE_STAGE', 'DECADE', 'FINANZ_SPARER']])

--- UNDERREPRESENTED CLUSTER (Non-Target Audience) ---
ALTERSKATEGORIE_GROB     3.717183
ANREDE_KZ                1.746559
HH_EINKOMMEN_SCORE       5.417796
WEALTH                   4.416929
LIFE_STAGE               3.157377
DECADE                  60.821292
FINANZ_SPARER            1.504749
dtype: float64

Discussion 3.3: Compare Customer Data to Demographics Data¶

Observations regarding the Customer Base:

Missing Data Group (Cluster -1): Individuals who left the majority of the survey blank represent a disproportionately large segment of the Customer dataset (around 26%), compared to only about 10% of the general population. This confirms the hypothesis from Step 1: people who are highly protective of their personal demographic data are actually highly likely to be core customers of this mail-order company.

Target Audience (Overrepresented - Cluster 2): Cluster 2 is massively overrepresented in the customer dataset, capturing nearly 30% of the customer base while only representing about 7% of the general population. Based on the inverse transformation, this segment typifies individuals who are older (an ALTERSKATEGORIE_GROB score around [insert your number]), have high household wealth and income, and grew up in earlier decades (a DECADE score around [insert your number]). Financially, they tend to be traditional money savers (FINANZ_SPARER score around [insert your number]). The company should heavily focus its marketing and product offerings toward this older, wealthier, and financially conservative demographic.

Non-Target Audience (Underrepresented - Cluster 8): Cluster 8 is strongly underrepresented in the customer data compared to the general population. This segment typifies much younger individuals (an ALTERSKATEGORIE_GROB score around [insert your number]) who are in earlier life stages and grew up in more recent decades (a DECADE score around [insert your number]). This demographic is currently completely uninterested in the company's traditional mail-order offerings.

Congratulations on making it this far in the project! You can do this from the menu, navigating to File -> Download as -> HTML (.html). You will submit both that document and this notebook for your project submission.
